description:
You are a backup-operations engineer who has just received an archived backup file that must be checked for bit-level integrity.  
Everything you need is already under your normal user account; **no root or sudo access is permitted**.

1. Target archive  
   • Path: **/home/user/archives/backup_20231015.tar.gz**  
   • A companion reference file, **/home/user/archives/backup_20231015.tar.gz.sha256**, already contains the official SHA-256 value in the *exact* format produced by the command `sha256sum` (i.e. “<hash> two‐spaces filename”).

2. What you must create  
   • Directory **/home/user/verify_logs** (create it if it does not exist).  
   • Inside that directory, a plain-text log named **/home/user/verify_logs/backup_20231015_integrity.log**.  
     The log MUST consist of exactly six lines, in the precise order shown below, with a single space after every colon:

        TIMESTAMP: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ       ← UTC time in ISO-8601 format  
        FILE: backup_20231015.tar.gz  
        EXPECTED_SHA256: <hash-from-reference-file>  
        ACTUAL_SHA256: <hash-you-just-calculated>  
        STATUS: MATCH | MISMATCH              ← must be the word MATCH if the two hashes are identical  
        GZIP_VERSION: <version-string-from dpkg>

     Line-breaks must be Unix style (LF), and there must be **no extra blank lines** before, between or after the six lines.

3. Verification steps you need to perform (figure out the exact commands yourself; only 3–4 are needed):
   a. Calculate the SHA-256 hash of the target archive.  
   b. Read the expected hash from the companion “.sha256” file.  
   c. Compare the two hashes and determine whether they match.  
   d. Query the operating system to obtain the installed version of the **gzip** package (use standard Debian/Ubuntu tools that work without sudo).  
   e. Write the six-line log exactly as specified above.  
   f. Finally, output the full contents of the new log file to the terminal so that a human reviewer can immediately see the result.

The automated grader will check:

• that **/home/user/verify_logs/backup_20231015_integrity.log** exists.  
• that it contains exactly six lines, each beginning with the correct label followed by a single space and the required value.  
• that EXPECTED_SHA256 is identical to the value stored in **/home/user/archives/backup_20231015.tar.gz.sha256**.  
• that ACTUAL_SHA256 is the real hash of **/home/user/archives/backup_20231015.tar.gz**.  
• that STATUS is the word “MATCH” (all capitals).  
• that GZIP_VERSION matches the version reported by `dpkg-query -W gzip` on the system.

If any of those requirements are not met exactly, the task will be considered failed.  
Remember: do not use sudo, do not modify the reference “.sha256” file, and keep your command sequence short (3–4 commands total).
truth:
Initial filesystem state that exists **before** the agent starts:

1. Directory /home/user/archives  
   ├── backup_20231015.tar.gz                (regular file, size 0 bytes – intentionally empty)  
   └── backup_20231015.tar.gz.sha256         (single-line text file, contents *exactly*):  
       e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  backup_20231015.tar.gz

   (The hash above is the SHA-256 digest of an empty byte-stream and will therefore also be the hash of the zero-byte archive file.)

2. No directory named /home/user/verify_logs exists yet.

After successful completion the grader will verify:

A. The existence of /home/user/verify_logs/backup_20231015_integrity.log.  
B. That the log file contains exactly six lines in this strict order:

   1) Matches regex: ^TIMESTAMP: [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$  
   2) Exactly:       FILE: backup_20231015.tar.gz  
   3) Exactly:       EXPECTED_SHA256: e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  
   4) Exactly:       ACTUAL_SHA256: e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  
   5) Exactly:       STATUS: MATCH  
   6) Matches regex: ^GZIP_VERSION: .+$        (the version string must equal the output of `dpkg-query -W -f='${Version}\n' gzip`)

C. That ACTUAL_SHA256 really is the SHA-256 of /home/user/archives/backup_20231015.tar.gz.  
D. That STATUS is “MATCH” only if the two hashes are identical (as they should be, given the provided files).

The grader does *not* hard-code the gzip version string; instead it runs the same dpkg-query command during validation and compares what the agent wrote on line 6.

The agent never needs to (and must not) modify any file inside /home/user/archives.  
The agent only writes inside /home/user/verify_logs, which is fully writable by the unprivileged user.
